info.title: Bisnode Company Risk and Credit Data API
info.description:
Bisnode Company Risk and Credit Data API.

Required scope to use this api: credit_data_companies

post.summary: Basic search on registrationNumber, dunsNumber and/or name.
post.description: A simple search on companies. Either registrationNumber, dunsNumber or name must be supplied. Country must also be set in the request.

post.summary: Fetch credit and risk data on a swedish company
post.description:
Fetch risk and credit data on swedish companies.
To get a pdf representation of the data set the Accept header to 'application/pdf'. You need to provide registrationNumber or dunsNumber.

451.description: Even though the company exist we can't deliver any data due to legal reasons.

post.summary: Fetch the articles of association report with the given registration number

To get a pdf representation of the data set the Accept header to 'application/pdf'.

rawScore.description: A value used to predict the likelihood that the risk will occur.

scoreValue.description:
A number that identifies where the entity's score falls relative to the scores of other entities.
For DK and FI: 
  the value is a percentile score. If the percentile here is 10, it means that this entity's score is the same as or better than 10% of the entities in the specific country, but not as good as 90% of the entities.
For NO and SE: 
  the 1-100 Score is derived based on the raw score and not based on percentages of the Data Cloud. This will mean that 1-100 score will not be 1% intervals based on volume, but rather a direct mapping to rawscore based on the certain Master Scale.
Min 0, max 100.

percentileScore.description:
A number that identifies where the entity's score falls relative to the scores of other entities.
For example, if the percentile here is 10, it means that this entity's score is the same as or better than 10% of the entities in the specific country, but not as good as 90% of the entities.
Min 0, max 100.

This field is deprecated. It was renamed as scoreValue. scoreValue and percentileScore contain the same value. We recommend using the new scoreValue field as percentileScore will be removed in a future API version.

probabilityOfDefault.description:
The likelihood that the inferred risk will occur for the entity.
Min 0, max 1.

riskPercentage.description:
The likelihood, expressed as a percentage, that the inferred risk will occur for the entity.
Min: 0%, max:100%.

ExclusionCode.title: ExclusionCode

code.description:
NO Exclusion Codes:     
  ASER - AS and ASA registered only in the Register of Local Entities.     
  AVDL - Business form - subunit.     
  BEIS - Reconstruction.     
  BRNR - Industries with the following NACE codes: 64110, 64190, 64201, 64301, 64305, 64910, 64920, 65110, 65120, 65200, 65300.     
  CEBR - Applies to sole proprietorship (ENK) where the business owner has an active credit excluding payment remark of the type: IS, KR, GF, FG, TG or MK.     
  DNR1 - Applies to sole proprietorship (ENK) where the business owner has temporary National Identification number (DNR).     
  FRAT - Negative creditor warning. One of the following: BOARD OF DIRECTORS RESIGNED, CHAIRMAN OF THE BOARD RESIGNED, THE COMPANY AUDITOR RESIGNED.     
  FREG - Errors in the accounts.     
  KONK - MK - bankruptcy.     
  LIKV - Liquidated.     
  MOPP - Negative creditor warning. VOLUNTARY WINDING-UP.     
  NEKV - Negative creditor warning. COMPULSORY WINDING-UP.     
  NGAH - Negative current event.     
  NOKM - Business without financial accounts (otherwise).     
  POST - Applies to sole proprietorship (ENK) where the business owner has a postal code 0000.     
  REGN - Financial Accounts status (had delivered financial accounts before, but not for the last 22 months) Does not apply for ANS/DA.     
  REKK - Reconstruction.     
  RGSD - Applies to sole proprietorship (ENK). The business owner is deceased or the business is without owner.     
  RMAS - Applies only for AS, ASA or STI business forms where financial accounts are missing. It does not apply to newly established companies with age less than 28 months.     
  SFOR - Business form is not scored.     
  SJON - Negative creditor warning. Merger or merger with another company.     
  SPRE - Special information registered with the company (credit block, available only at ENK).     
  TVOP - TA - compulsory winding up.     
  UTVA - Sole proprietorship where business owner is immigrated. 

SE Exclusion Codes:     
  K - Bankruptcy.     
  U - Distraint.     
  S - Debt balance, Private claims.     
  L - Liquidation.     
  B - Disqualified for running a company.     
  e - Part owner.     
  g - Reconstruction initiated.     
  b - Shared capital consumed.     
  J - Parent company in bankruptcy.     
  I - Subsidiary in bankruptcy.     
  D - Deceased.     
  H - Discontinued.     
  A - Deasserted.     
  O - Underage.     
  R - Deregistered.     
  7 - Not an active company.     
  s - Score can not be calculated (Manual judgement).     
  M - Merger concluded.     
  r - Bankruptcy petition.     
  f - Shelf company.     
  F - Company missing.     
  a - Lack of information in annual accounts.     
  E - Emigrated.     
  V - Trustee.     
  P - Person is missing.     
  X - Person blocked.     
  Z - Annual account not updated.     
  Y - Company blocked.     
  m - Run on commission.     
  n - Auditor comment cancels control balance sheet.     
  p - Winding unjunction within 6 months.     
  BIC - There is no score calculation for Bank and Insurance companies.     
  A company can have multiple exclusion codes at the same time. You will get all of them unordered. If you need the one with highest priority consider the following order of priority: K c U S L B e g b J I D H A O R 7 s M r f a E V P X Z Y m n p.     

  FI Exclusion Codes:     
  1881 - Out of Business.     
  2233 - Events - Bankruptcy or similar business failure proceedings.     
  36702 - Business is subject to a voluntary dissolution application.     
  2345 - Operating Status - Inactive/Inoperative.     
  41937 - Reconstruction opened.     
  42206 - Model Exclusion based on Legal Structure.     
  35740 - Company operating in financial/insurance industry.     
  11997 - Risk Score Unavailable - Contact D&B Customer Services.     
  15807 - Financial Information is not available for this business.     
  1661 - Minimum Data.     

DK Exclusion Codes:     
  GEC - Excluded from score calculation.

DanishBalanceSheet.title: DanishBalanceSheet
DanishBalanceSheet.description: A balance sheet is a financial statement that reports a company's assets, liabilities and shareholders' equity at a specific point in time, and provides a basis for computing rates of return and evaluating its capital structure. It is a financial statement that provides a snapshot of what a company owns and owes, as well as the amount invested by shareholders.

DanishAssets.title: DanishAssets
DanishAssets.description: Assets are things that the company owns. They are the resources of the company that have been acquired through transactions, and have future economic value that can be measured and expressed in monetary values. Assets also include costs paid in advance that have not yet expired, such as prepaid advertising, prepaid insurance, prepaid legal fees, and prepaid rent.

DanishCurrentAssets.title: DanishCurrentAssets

inventories.description: Inventories represents raw materials, components and finished products.

workInProgress.description: Work in progress (WIP) refers to partially-completed goods that are still in the production process. These items do not include raw materials or finished goods. Work in progress is usually comprised of the full amount of raw materials required for a product, since that is added at the beginning of production, plus the cost of additional processing as each unit progresses through the various manufacturing steps.

accountsReceivable.description: Accounts receivable is the balance of money due to a firm for goods or services delivered or used but not yet paid for by customers.

receivableFromGroupAndAssociatedCompanie.description: Short term receivable from group and associated companies.

accruedRevenuesAndDeferredExpenses.description: Deferred revenue is the portion of a company's revenue that has not been earned, but cash has been collected from customers in the form of a prepayment. Accrued expenses are the expenses of a company that have been incurred but not yet paid.

securities.description: Marketable securities are a type of liquid asset on the balance sheet of a financial report, meaning they can easily be converted to cash. They include holdings such as stocks, bonds, and other securities that are bought and sold daily.

cashAndCashEquivalents.description: Cash and cash equivalents refers to the line item on the balance sheet that reports the value of a company's assets that are cash or can be converted into cash immediately. These include bank accounts, marketable securities, commercial paper, Treasury bills, and short-term government bonds with a maturity date of three months or less. Marketable securities and money market holdings are considered cash equivalents because they are liquid and not subject to material fluctuations in value.

otherCurrentAssets.description: Other current assets (OCA) is a category of things of value that a company owns, benefits from, or uses to generate income that can be converted into cash within one business cycle. They are referred to as “other” because they are uncommon or insignificant, unlike typical current asset items such as cash, securities, accounts receivable, inventory, and prepaid expenses.

currentAssetsTotal.description: Current assets include cash, cash equivalents, accounts receivable, stock inventory, marketable securities, pre-paid liabilities, and other liquid assets.

DanishCurrentAssets.description: Cash and other resources that are expected to turn to cash or to be used up within one year of the balance sheet date. (If a company's operating cycle is longer than one year, an item is a current asset if it will turn to cash or be used up within the operating cycle.) Current assets are presented in the order of liquidity, i.e., cash, temporary investments, accounts receivable, inventory, supplies, prepaid insurance.

DanishNonCurrentAssets.title: DanishNonCurrentAssets

goodwill.description: Goodwill represents values that arises when you buy a company. If you pay a price that is higher than the value of the assets in the company the remaining amount is concidered as goodwill. Goodwill can be a subject of depreciations. It is an intangible asset.

capitalisedCostForRnd.description: Capitalized cost for Research and Development.

intangibleRights.description: Concessions, patents, licenses, trademarks and similar rights.

otherIntangibleAssets.description: These assets could be lease contracts, work in progress, intellectual property rights or anything that is not tangible.

intangibleAssetsTotal.description: Total intangible assets. Intangible assets are economic resources that have no physical presence. They include patents, trademarks, copyrights, and goodwill. Accounting for intangible assets differs depending on the type of asset, and they can be either amortized or tested for impairment each year.

landAndBuildings.description: This is property or real estate. There are many kinds of property like land buildings, golf courses, cabins, mining areas etc.

otherTangibleAssets.description: Tangible assets not being any of the above, and is not a subject for depreciation. For example art or any other item with a remaining value over time. Even fixed assets under production, for example an ongoing construction could be placed here.

nonCurrentAssetsTotal.description:
Noncurrent assets are a company’s long-term investments where the full value will not be realized within the accounting year. 
  Non-current assets can be considered anything not classified as current.

DanishNonCurrentAssets.description: A noncurrent asset is an asset that is not expected to be consumed within one year. If a company has a high proportion of noncurrent to current assets, this can be an indicator of poor liquidity, since a large amount of cash may be needed to support ongoing investments in noncash assets.

DanishLiabilities.title: DanishLiabilities
DanishLiabilities.description: Liabilities are the money that a company owes to outside parties, from bills it has to pay to suppliers to interest on bonds it has issued to creditors to rent, utilities and salaries.

DanishCurrentLiabilities.title: DanishCurrentLiabilities

liabilitiesToGroupCompaniesAndAssociates.description: Short-term liabilities to group companies and associates.

accountsPayable.description: Accounts payable is an accounting entry representing a company's obligation to pay off a short-term debt to its creditors or suppliers.

DanishCurrentLiabilities.description: Current liabilities are a company's debts or obligations that are due within one year or within a normal operating cycle. Furthermore, current liabilities are settled by the use of a current asset, such as cash, or by creating a new current liability.

DanishLongTermLiabilities.title: DanishLongTermLiabilities

liabilitiesToGroupCompaniesAndAssociates.description: Long-term liabilities to group companies and associates.

DanishLongTermLiabilities.description: Long-term liabilities are financial obligations of a company that are due more than one year in the future. The current portion of long-term debt is listed separately to provide a more accurate view of a company's current liquidity and the company’s ability to pay current liabilities as they become due. Long-term liabilities are also called long-term debt or noncurrent liabilities.

DanishShareholdersEquity.title: DanishShareholdersEquity

shareCapital.description: Share capital refers to funds raised by issuing shares in return for cash or other considerations.

revaluationReserve.description: Revaluation reserve is an accounting term used when a company creates a line item on its balance sheet to account for asset value fluctuations.

DanishShareholdersEquity.description: Shareholders' equity is the money attributable to a business' owners, meaning its shareholders. It is also known as "net assets," since it is equivalent to the total assets of a company minus its liabilities, that is, the debt it owes to non-shareholders.

netSales.description: Net sales - sales from the companys main industry or industries.

changeInInventories.description: Inventory change is the difference between the inventory totals for the last reporting period and the current reporting period.

externalInterestIncome.description: Interest payed to the company by banks or creditinstitutes.

otherOperatingIncome.description: Other operating income includes revenue from all other operating activities which are not related to the principal activities of the company, such as gains/losses from disposals, interest income, dividend income, etc.

costOfGoods.description: Cost of goods sold (COGS) refers to the direct costs attributable to the production of the goods sold in a company. This amount includes the cost of the materials used in creating the good along with the direct labor costs used to produce the good. It excludes indirect expenses, such as distribution costs and sales force costs.

sellingExpenses.description: Selling expense is reported on the income statement as the sum of all direct and indirect selling expenses of a company. It includes all the costs not directly tied to making a product or performing a service.

administrativeCosts.description: Administrative expenses/costs are the expenses that an organization incurs not directly tied to a specific function such as manufacturing, production or sales. These expenses are related to the organization as a whole as opposed to an individual department.

researchAndDevelopment.description: Research and development (R&D) expenses are associated with the research and development of a company's goods or services. A company generally incurs R&D expenses in the process of finding and creating new products or services.

staffCosts.description: Staff costs naturally include salary costs (including social security contributions) but also Recruitment costs, training costs, Costs for staff health care, pension costs and Business health care costs.

depreciation.description: Depreciation is an accounting method of allocating the cost of a tangible asset over its useful life and is used to account for declines in value. Businesses depreciate long-term assets for both tax and accounting purposes.

operatingProfitAfterDepreciation.description: Operating profit is an accounting figure that measures the profit earned from a company's ongoing core business operations, thus excluding deductions of interest and taxes. This value also does not include any profit earned from the firm's investments, such as earnings from firms in which the company has partial interest.

otherFinancialIncome.description: Income for a company that comes from anything other than its ordinary operations.

externalInterestExpenses.description: Interest income from group companies. Example- Bank loans ?

otherFinancialExpenses.description: Other financial expenses not covered by externalInterestExpenses and interestExpenseToGroupCompanies.

financialCostsTotal.description: Total financial costs. A sum of all costs.

extraordinaryIncome.description: Extraordinary income is an unusual earning that results from unusual circumstances unlikely to be repeated. For example, if a corporation sells off idle real estate, this would generate income for the company, but it would be extraordinary, because it’s unlikely to keep selling real estate on a regular basis.

extraordinaryExpenses.description: Extraordinary expenses is unusual expenses that results from unusual circumstances unlikely to be repeated.

extraordinaryItemsTotal.description:
Extraordinary items consisted of gains or losses from events that were unusual and infrequent in nature that were separately classified, presented, and disclosed on companies' financial statements.
extraordinaryIncome - extraordinaryExpenses = extraordinaryItemsTotal

taxOnAnnualResult.description: Tax on profit or loss for the year

annualResult.description: Profit or loss for the year

fiscalPeriod.description: Fiscal year-end refers to the completion of a one-year, or 12-month, accounting period. A fiscal year is the period used for calculating annual financial statements. A company's fiscal year may differ from the calendar year, and may not close on December 31 due to the nature of a company's needs.

currency.description: Currency expressed with the ISO standard 4217, see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_4217. Applies for all fields in this block and the ones below containg monetary values.

netSales.description: Net sales - sales from the companies main industry or industries.

employeeCount.description: The number of employees at the time of this annual report.

equityAssetsRatio.description:
Calculated equity in percent of the total assets.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

quickRatio.description:
Current assets minus inventories in percent of current liabilities.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

currentRatio.description:
Current assets in percent of Short term liabilities.
The current ratio is a liquidity ratio that measures whether or not a firm has enough resources to meet its short-term obligations. It compares a firm's current assets to its current liabilities

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

profitMargin.description:
Operating profit/loss plus financial income as a percentage of turnover.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

returnOnTotalAssets.description:
Profit/loss after financial items plus financial costs as a percentage of the total capital.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

returnOnEquity.description:
Profit/loss after financial items as a percentage of the total equity.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

liabilityRatio.description:
Liabilities in percent of equity.
The liabilities to assets (L/A) ratio is a solvency ratio that examines how much of a company's assets are made of liabilities. A ratio of 20 percent means that 20 percent of the company are liabilities.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

turnoverAssets.description:
Capital turnover is measured as profit/loss in relation to assets.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

grossMargin.description:
Gross margin is a company's net sales revenue minus its cost of goods sold (COGS). The gross margin represents the amount of sales revenue that the company retains after incurring the direct costs associated with producing the goods and services it sells. The higher the amount, the more the company retains on each dollar of sales to service its other costs and debt obligations. A firm's net sales is gross revenue less returns, allowances, and discounts.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

growthInNetResult.description:
Growth in net value of an organisation's assets, after deducting any liabilities, compared to the result from the previous statement.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

growthInTurnover.description:
Growth in turnover

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

growthInGrossProfit.description:
Growth in gross profit

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

growthInPrimaryResult.description:
Growth in primary result

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

growthInPretaxResult.description:
Growth in pree-tax result

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

growthInProfitAndLossAccount.description:
Growth in profit and loss account

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

capacityRatio.description:
Capacity Ratio

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

safetyMargin.description:
The difference between actual sales and break-even sales.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

daysOfPayables.description:
The average time (in days) that a company takes to pay its bills and invoices to its trade creditors, which include suppliers, vendors or other companies.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

daysOfReceivables.description:
A measure of the average number of days that it takes a company to collect payment after a sale has been made. 

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

inventoriesToTurnover.description:
The ratio is used for investigating how much of the capital is tied up in inventories. A high value indicates that the turnover of the stock is slow.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

equityOfTurnover.description:
Shows in '%'

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

liquidAssetsOfTurnover.description:
liquid assets in percent of total turnover.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

grossMarginInPercent.description:
Shows in '%'

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

gearing.description:
Gearing refers to the relationship, or ratio, of a company's debt to equity. Gearing shows the extent to which a firm's operations are funded by lenders versus shareholders—in other words, it measures a company’s financial leverage. 

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

profitRate.description:
Profit Rate. Shows in '%'.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

payoutRatio.description:
The payout ratio shows the proportion of earnings paid out as dividends to shareholders, typically expressed as a percentage of the company's earnings. The payout ratio can also be expressed as dividends paid out as a proportion of cash flow. The payout ratio is also known as the dividend payout ratio.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

description.description: Code description in selected language.

FinnishBalanceSheet.title: BalanceSheet
FinnishBalanceSheet.description: A balance sheet is a financial statement that reports a company's assets, liabilities and shareholders' equity at a specific point in time, and provides a basis for computing rates of return and evaluating its capital structure. It is a financial statement that provides a snapshot of what a company owns and owes, as well as the amount invested by shareholders.

FinnishAssets.title: Assets
FinnishAssets.description: Assets are things that the company owns. They are the resources of the company that have been acquired through transactions, and have future economic value that can be measured and expressed in monetary values. Assets also include costs paid in advance that have not yet expired, such as prepaid advertising, prepaid insurance, prepaid legal fees, and prepaid rent.

FinnishCurrentAssets.title: FinnishCurrentAssets

workInProgress.description: Work in progress (WIP) refers to partially-completed goods that are still in the production process. These items do not include raw materials or finished goods. Work in progress is usually comprised of the full amount of raw materials required for a product, since that is added at the beginning of production, plus the cost of additional processing as each unit progresses through the various manufacturing steps.

propertyForSale.description: Estimated value for properties that are to be sold.

accountsReceivable.description: Accounts receivable is the balance of money due to a firm for goods or services delivered or used but not yet paid for by customers.

otherCurrentAssets.description: Other current assets (OCA) is a category of things of value that a company owns, benefits from, or uses to generate income that can be converted into cash within one business cycle. They are referred to as “other” because they are uncommon or insignificant, unlike typical current asset items such as cash, securities, accounts receivable, inventory, and prepaid expenses.

currentAssetsTotal.description: Current assets include cash, cash equivalents, accounts receivable, stock inventory, marketable securities, pre-paid liabilities, and other liquid assets.

FinnishCurrentAssets.description: Cash and other resources that are expected to turn to cash or to be used up within one year of the balance sheet date. (If a company's operating cycle is longer than one year, an item is a current asset if it will turn to cash or be used up within the operating cycle.) Current assets are presented in the order of liquidity, i.e., cash, temporary investments, accounts receivable, inventory, supplies, prepaid insurance.

FinnishNonCurrentAssets.title: FinnishNonCurrentAssets

capitalisedCostForRnd.description: Capitalized cost for Research and Development projects.

intangibleRights.description: Concessions, patents, licenses, trademarks and similar rights.

goodwill.description: Goodwill represents values that arises when you buy a company. If you pay a price that is higher than the value of the assets in the company the remaining amount is concidered as goodwill. Goodwill can be a subject of depreciations. It is an intangible asset.

otherIntangibleAssets.description: These assets could be lease contracts, work in progress, intellectual property rights or anything that is not tangible.

intangibleAssetsTotal.description: Total intangible assets.. Intangible assets are economic resources that have no physical presence. They include patents, trademarks, copyrights, and goodwill. Accounting for intangible assets differs depending on the type of asset, and they can be either amortized or tested for impairment each year.

landAndBuildings.description: This is property or real estate. There are many kinds of property like land buildings, golf courses, cabins, mining areas etc.

otherTangibleAssets.description: Tangible assets not being any of the above, and is not a subject for depreciation. For example art or any other item with a remaining value over time. Even fixed assets under production, for example an ongoing construction could be placed here.

ownShares.description: The value of the shares the company owns in their own company.

nonCurrentAssetsTotal.description:
Noncurrent assets are a company’s long-term investments where the full value will not be realized within the accounting year. 
  Non-current assets can be considered anything not classified as current.

FinnishNonCurrentAssets.description: A noncurrent asset is an asset that is not expected to be consumed within one year. If a company has a high proportion of noncurrent to current assets, this can be an indicator of poor liquidity, since a large amount of cash may be needed to support ongoing investments in noncash assets.

FinnishLiabilities.title: FinnishLiabilities
FinnishLiabilities.description: Liabilities are the money that a company owes to outside parties, from bills it has to pay to suppliers to interest on bonds it has issued to creditors to rent, utilities and salaries.

FinnishCurrentLiabilities.title: FinnishCurrentLiabilities

accountsPayable.description: Accounts payable is an accounting entry representing a company's obligation to pay off a short-term debt to its creditors or suppliers.

FinnishCurrentLiabilities.description: Current liabilities are a company's debts or obligations that are due within one year or within a normal operating cycle. Furthermore, current liabilities are settled by the use of a current asset, such as cash, or by creating a new current liability.

FinnishLongTermLiabilities.title: FinnishLongTermLiabilities

longTermLoansFromPensionInstitutions.description: Long term loans from pension institutions.

FinnishLongTermLiabilities.description: Long-term liabilities are financial obligations of a company that are due more than one year in the future. The current portion of long-term debt is listed separately to provide a more accurate view of a company's current liquidity and the company’s ability to pay current liabilities as they become due. Long-term liabilities are also called long-term debt or noncurrent liabilities.

FinnishShareholdersEquity.title: FinnishShareholdersEquity

shareCapital.description: Share capital refers to funds raised by issuing shares in return for cash or other considerations.

sharePremiumAccount.description: A share premium account is the amount of money paid for a share above the cost of the share.

revaluationReserve.description: Revaluation reserve is an accounting term used when a company creates a line item on its balance sheet to account for asset value fluctuations.

equityTotal.description:
Total equity.
Equity is typically referred to as shareholder equity (also known as shareholders' equity) which represents the amount of money that would be returned to a company’s shareholders if all of the assets were liquidated and all of the company's debt was paid off.

FinnishShareholdersEquity.description: Shareholders' equity is the money attributable to a business' owners, meaning its shareholders. It is also known as "net assets," since it is equivalent to the total assets of a company minus its liabilities, that is, the debt it owes to non-shareholders.

changeInInventories.description: Change in inventories and work in progress for the last reporting period and the current reporting period.

workPerformedForOwnUse.description: Work performed by the company for its own use and capitalized here when reported as assets.

otherOperatingIncome.description: Other operation income that doesn't count to the normal sales, for example rental income for companys that doesn't conduct property management.

turnoverTotal.description: The company’s total revenues. Net sales + otherOperatingIncome

costOfGoods.description: Cost of goods sold (COGS) refers to the direct costs attributable to the production of the goods sold in a company. This amount includes the cost of the materials used in creating the good along with the direct labor costs used to produce the good. It excludes indirect expenses, such as distribution costs and sales force costs.

staffCosts.description: Staff costs naturally include salary costs (including social security contributions) but also Recruitment costs, training costs, Costs for staff health care, pension costs and Business health care costs.

resultBeforeExtraordinaryItems.description: An extraordinary item is an event or transaction that is considered abnormal, not related to ordinary company activities, and unlikely to recur in the foreseeable future.

otherOperatingExpenses.description: Costs outside of the companys main business and that appropriately can't be placed in other posts in the income statement.

depreciation.description: Depreciation is an accounting method of allocating the cost of a tangible asset over its useful life and is used to account for declines in value. Businesses depreciate long-term assets for both tax and accounting purposes.

externalInterestIncome.description: Interest payed to the company by banks or creditinstitutes.

otherFinancialIncome.description: Financial income that is not interest or profit participation from affiliate. For example dividend, profit from bonds or result from stock trading.

externalInterestExpenses.description: Interest payed from the company to banks or creditinstitutes.

otherFinancialExpenses.description: Other financial expenses not covered by externalInterestExpenses and interestExpenseToGroupCompanies.

operatingResult.description: The operating result shows what the company has earned in connection with its business activities, that is to say before deduction of interest and taxes

extraordinaryIncome.description: Extraordinary income is an unusual earning that results from unusual circumstances unlikely to be repeated. For example, if a corporation sells off idle real estate, this would generate income for the company, but it would be extraordinary, because it’s unlikely to keep selling real estate on a regular basis.

extraordinaryExpenses.description: Extraordinary expenses is unusual expenses that results from unusual circumstances unlikely to be repeated.

netProfitLoss.description: A positive value means that the company made a net profit and a negative that it made a net loss.

fiscalPeriod.description: Fiscal year-end refers to the completion of a one-year, or 12-month, accounting period. A fiscal year is the period used for calculating annual financial statements. A company's fiscal year may differ from the calendar year, and may not close on December 31 due to the nature of a company's needs.

currency.description: Currency expressed with the ISO standard 4217, see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_4217. Applies for all fields in this block and the ones below containg monetary values.

currentRatio.description:
Current assets in percent of Short term liabilities.

The current ratio is a liquidity ratio that measures whether or not a firm has enough resources to meet its short-term obligations. It compares a firm's current assets to its current liabilities

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

equityAssetsRatio.description:
Calculated equity in percent of the total assets.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

gearing.description:
Gearing refers to the relationship, or ratio, of a company's debt to equity. Gearing shows the extent to which a firm's operations are funded by lenders versus shareholders—in other words, it measures a company’s financial leverage. 

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

liabilityRatio.description:
Liabilities in percent of equity.

The liabilities to assets (L/A) ratio is a solvency ratio that examines how much of a company's assets are made of liabilities. A ratio of 20 percent means that 20 percent of the company are liabilities.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

profitMargin.description:
profit rate in brigs web
Operating profit/loss plus financial income as a percentage of turnover.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

quickRatio.description:
Current assets minus inventories in percent of current liabilities.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

returnOnEquity.description:
Profit/loss after financial items as a percentage of the total equity.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

returnOnInvestments.description:
Return on Investment (ROI) is a performance measure used to evaluate the efficiency of an investment or compare the efficiency of a number of different investments. ROI tries to directly measure the amount of return on a particular investment, relative to the investment’s cost. To calculate ROI, the benefit (or return) of an investment is divided by the cost of the investment. The result is expressed as a percentage or a ratio.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

returnOnTotalAssets.description:
Return on assets (ROA) is an indicator of how profitable a company is relative to its total assets. ROA gives a manager, investor, or analyst an idea as to how efficient a company's management is at using its assets to generate earnings. Return on assets is displayed as a percentage.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

turnover.description: The company’s total revenues. Net sales + otherOperatingIncome

assetsTotal.description: An asset is any resource owned by the business. Anything tangible or intangible that can be owned or controlled to produce value and that is held by a company to produce positive economic value is an asset.

equityTotal.description:
Total equity.
Equity is typically referred to as shareholder equity (also known as shareholders' equity) which represents the amount of money that would be returned to a company’s shareholders if all of the assets were liquidated and all of the company's debt was paid off.

daysOfPayables.description:
The average time (in days) that a company takes to pay its bills and invoices to its trade creditors, which include suppliers, vendors or other companies.

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

daysOfReceivables.description:
A measure of the average number of days that it takes a company to collect payment after a sale has been made. 

Formatted using period as the decimal point.

FinnishFloatingCharge.title: FinnishFloatingCharge

reference.description: reference id of the floating charge

FinnishFloatingCharge.description: A floating charge, also known as a floating lien, is a security interest or lien over a group of non-constant assets. The assets may change in quantity and value. Companies will use floating charges as a means of securing a loan. Typically, a loan might be secured by fixed assets such as property or equipment, but with a floating charge, the underlying assets are usually current assets or short-term assets that can change in value.

count.description: Total number of subsidiaries this company have.

FinnishSubsidiary.title: FinnishSubsidiary
FinnishSubsidiary.description: A company relates to another one as it's subsidiary as and when that other company owns the majority (>50%) of the capital of the company in focus.

FamilyTree.title: FamilyTree
FamilyTree.description: A view of the owner structure.

ownershipInPercent.description: Ownership in percent, formatted using period as the decimal point.

SwedishPetition.description: A Petition to pay is sent in by the creditor to the Enforcement agency when a debtor does not pay in due time. Reminders can be sent first. The Enforcement agency act on behalf of the creditor, it does not take over the debt. The enforcement agency sends out a letter to the debtor saying that there is a debt which should be paid. The Debtor signs the note and sends it back. The debtor can protest or pay. If protested on, the case is sent to court and credit info companies will not get more information except if there is a default judgement. There are petitions sent to persons but credit info companies does only receive petitions where the debtor are organizations.

auditorsReservationCode.description:
A  -  AT LEAST ONE OF FOLLOWING THREE CRITERIA IS NOT RECOMMENDED BY THE AUDITOR:                                                               
       1. RECOMMENDATION THAT THE INCOME STATEMENT AND BALANCE SHEET COULD BE ADOPTED.
       2. THE UNAPPROPRIATED RESULT IS DEALT WITH IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE PROPOSAL IN THE ADMINISTRATION REPORT.
      3. THE BOARD MEMBERS AND MANAGING DIRECTOR ARE DISCHARGED FROM LIABILITY FOR THE FINANCIAL YEAR.                                       
B  -  AT LEAST ONE BOARD MEMBER, OR THE MANAGING DIRECTOR, IS DISCHARGED FROM LIABILITY FOR THE FINANCIAL YEAR.                    
C  -  BALANCE SHEET FOR LIQUIDATION PURPOSES IS CREATED WHICH  SHOWS THAT THE COMPANY IS STILL BOUND TO LIQUIDATION.     DEF. BOUND TO LIQUIDATION; SHAREHOLDERS EQUITY <50% OF SHARE CAPITAL.                                 
D  -  AUDITOR DECLARE BOARDMEMBERS RESPONSIBILITY TO CREATE BALANCE SHEET FOR LIQUIDATION PURPOSES THEN COMPANY'S TOTAL CAPITAL IS < 50 % OF SHARE CAPITAL.                                                             
E  -  THE INCOME STATEMENT OR/AND BALANCE SHEET CONTAINS UNCERTAIN OR INCORRECT VALUED ITEMS.                     
F  -  DEFICIENCY IN THE COMPANY'S INTERNAL CONTROL.                              
G  -  TAXES HAVE NOT BEEN PAID PROPERLY.                                         
H  -  BALANCE SHEET FOR LIQUIDATION PURPOSES IS CREATED WHICH SHOWS THAT THE COMPANY IS NOT BOUND TO LIQUIDATION.DEF. BOUND TO LIQUIDATION; SHAREHOLDERS EQUITY < 50 % OF SHARE CAPITAL.                               
I  -  UNLEGAL LOAN TO SHAREHOLDER(S).                                            
J  -  COMMENTS FROM THE AUDITOR.                                                 
K  -  GENERAL INFORMATION OR COMMENT FROM THE AUDITOR IN THE ANNUAL REPORT.      
L  -  AT LEAST ONE OF FOLLOWING 3 CRITERIA IS NOT RECOMMENDED BY THE AUDITOR, CONCERNING THE GROUP:                                             
       1. RECOMMENDATION THAT THE CONSOLIDATED INCOME STATEMENT AND BALANCE SHEET COULD BE ADOPTED.                                         
       2. THE UNAPPROPRIATED RESULT IS DEALT WITH IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE PROPOSAL IN THE ADMINISTRATION REPORT.                              
       3. THE BOARD MEMBERS AND MANAGING DIRECTOR ARE DISCHARGED FROM LIABILITY FOR THE FINANCIAL YEAR.                                       
M  -  SHAREHOLDERS WILL GUARANTEE CURRENT CAPITAL CONTRIBUTIONS IN ORDER TO COVER SHORT AND LONG-TERM FINANCING.                                       
P  -  ANNUAL REPORT IS NOT SENT TO COMPANY HOUSE WITHIN STIPULATED TIME ALT. ANNUAL GENERAL MEETING NOT HELD IN ACCORDANCE WITH SWEDISH COMPANY ACT.    
R  -  COMMENT FROM THE AUDITOR.                                                  
1   -  CONCRETE OBSERVATIONS ON FLAWS IN ACCOUNTING PRACTICES                     
2   -  CONDITIONAL FINDINGS DUE TO INADEQUATE REPORTING                           
3   -  CONDITIONAL FINDINGS DUE TO INADEQUATCIES AND FLAWS                        
4   -  NEGATIVE OBSERVATIONS (IN THE ACCOUNTING PRACTICES EMPLOYED)               
5   -  THE AUDITOR REFRAINS FROM EXPRESSING HIS/HER OPINION  (DUE TO LACK/INACCESSIBILITY OF INFORMATION)            
6   -  UNSECURED LOANS TO SHAREHOLDERS                                            
7   -  WITHELD TAXES FROM EMPLOYEES ARE NOT DEPOSITED IN A SEPARATE ACCOUNT
8   -  LACK OF SUPPORTING DOCUMENTS ON OFFICIAL TRANSACTIONS AND POOR INTERNAL CONTROL PROCEDURES
9   -  FULL/PARTIAL SHARE CAPITAL IS LOST. FUTURE OPERATION DEPEND ON AVAILABILITY OF ADDITIONAL CAPITAL AND/OR IMPROVMENT IN OPERATING/NET RESULTS

The gross margin represents the amount of sales revenue that the company retains after incurring the direct costs associated with producing the goods and services it sells. The higher the amount, the more the company retains on each dollar of sales to service its other costs and debt obligations. A firm's net sales is gross revenue less returns, allowances, and discounts.

equity.description: Equity is typically referred to as shareholder equity (also known as shareholders' equity) which represents the amount of money that would be returned to a company’s shareholders if all of the assets were liquidated and all of the company's debt was paid off.

assetsTotal.description: An asset is any resource owned by the business. Anything tangible or intangible that can be owned or controlled to produce value and that is held by a company to produce positive economic value is an asset.

fiscalPeriod.description: Fiscal year-end refers to the completion of a one-year, or 12-month, accounting period. A fiscal year is the period used for calculating annual financial statements. A company's fiscal year may differ from the calendar year, and may not close on December 31 due to the nature of a company's needs.

SwedishBalanceSheet.title: SwedishBalanceSheet
SwedishBalanceSheet.description: A balance sheet is a financial statement that reports a company's assets, liabilities and shareholders' equity at a specific point in time, and provides a basis for computing rates of return and evaluating its capital structure. It is a financial statement that provides a snapshot of what a company owns and owes, as well as the amount invested by shareholders.

SwedishAssets.title: SwedishAssets
SwedishAssets.description: Assets are things that the company owns. They are the resources of the company that have been acquired through transactions, and have future economic value that can be measured and expressed in monetary values. Assets also include costs paid in advance that have not yet expired, such as prepaid advertising, prepaid insurance, prepaid legal fees, and prepaid rent.

SwedishCurrentAssets.title: SwedishCurrentAssets

workInProgress.description: Work in progress (WIP) refers to partially-completed goods that are still in the production process. These items do not include raw materials or finished goods. Work in progress is usually comprised of the full amount of raw materials required for a product, since that is added at the beginning of production, plus the cost of additional processing as each unit progresses through the various manufacturing steps.

otherInventories.description: Summation of stock in trade from the annual report. Stock in trade includes raw materials and consumables, work in progress, finished goods and goods for resale.

inventoriesTotal.description: The sum of the total inventories.

accountsReceivable.description: Accounts receivable is the balance of money due to a firm for goods or services delivered or used but not yet paid for by customers.

receivableFromGroupAndAssociatedCompanie.description: Current receivables within the same group company or associated company

otherReceivables.description: Other current receivables includes for example bills receivables and client funds.

currentReceivablesTotal.description: The sum of all current receivables.

currentInvestmentsTotal.description: Here the company accounts for funds that have been placed on a very short term, one to three days. In differ to the post cash bank theese funds are not immediately available in the business.

cashBankTotal.description: Assets that immediately can be used as means of payment.

otherCurrentAssets.description: Other current assets (OCA) is a category of things of value that a company owns, benefits from, or uses to generate income that can be converted into cash within one business cycle. They are referred to as “other” because they are uncommon or insignificant, unlike typical current asset items such as cash, securities, accounts receivable, inventory, and prepaid expenses.

currentAssetsTotal.description: Current assets include cash, cash equivalents, accounts receivable, stock inventory, marketable securities, pre-paid liabilities, and other liquid assets.

SwedishCurrentAssets.description: Cash and other resources that are expected to turn to cash or to be used up within one year of the balance sheet date. (If a company's operating cycle is longer than one year, an item is a current asset if it will turn to cash or be used up within the operating cycle.) Current assets are presented in the order of liquidity, i.e., cash, temporary investments, accounts receivable, inventory, supplies, prepaid insurance.

SwedishNonCurrentAssets.title: SwedishNonCurrentAssets

claimOnPayment.description: Registered, but not yet paid share capital

capitalisedCostForRnd.description: Capitalized cost for Research and Development.

intangibleRights.description: Concessions, patents, licenses, trademarks and similar rights.

goodwill.description: Goodwill represents values that arises when you buy a company. If you pay a price that is higher than the value of the assets in the company the remaining amount is concidered as goodwill. Goodwill can be a subject of depreciations. It is an intangible asset.

otherIntangibleAssets.description: These assets could be lease contracts, work in progress, intellectual property rights or anything that is not tangible.

intangibleAssetsTotal.description: Total intangible assets. Intangible assets are economic resources that have no physical presence. They include patents, trademarks, copyrights, and goodwill. Accounting for intangible assets differs depending on the type of asset, and they can be either amortized or tested for impairment each year.

landAndBuildings.description: This is property or real estate. There are many kinds of property like land buildings, golf courses, cabins, mining areas etc.

includingLand.description: Land is real estate or property, minus buildings and equipment.

machinery.description: Machinery are such tangible fixed assets that are intended to serve the business production.

inventory.description: Inventory is the term for the goods available for sale and raw materials used to produce goods available for sale.

machineryAndInventoryTotal.description: The sum of the posts machinery and inventories, is used in the annual report when these values can't be separated.

otherTangibleAssetsNonDepreciable.description: Tangible assets not being any of the above, and is not a subject for depreciation. For example art or any other item with a remaining value over time. Even fixed assets under production, for example an ongoing construction could be placed here.

otherTangibleFixedAssetsDepreciable.description: Tangible assets that is a subject for depreciation.

tangibleNonCurrentAssetsTotal.description: The sum of the companys physical tangible fixed assets. Land and buildings, machinery and inventories and other tangible fixed assets.

sharesInGroupCompaniesAndAssociates.description: Participation or shares in a subsidiary or associated company.

receivableFromGroupCompaniesAndAssociate.description: Receivables on companies in group or associated company.

loansToShareholdersAndRelatedParty.description: Unusual post to occur since it is not allowed, according to ABL, with loans to related party (CEO or/and board).

otherNonCurrentAssets.description: Other non-current assets that is not specified as receivables from/shares in group companies or loans to shareholders/related party.

financialNonCurrentAssetsTotal.description: The sum of all financial assets

nonCurrentAssetsTotal.description:
Noncurrent assets are a company’s long-term investments where the full value will not be realized within the accounting year. 
  Non-current assets can be considered anything not classified as current.

whereofNotDepreciableNonCurrentCosts.description: Whereof not depreciable non-current costs. Refers to Total non current assets.

SwedishNonCurrentAssets.description: A noncurrent asset is an asset that is not expected to be consumed within one year. If a company has a high proportion of noncurrent to current assets, this can be an indicator of poor liquidity, since a large amount of cash may be needed to support ongoing investments in noncash assets.

equityAndLiabilitiesTotal.description: The sum of all reported liabilities.

SwedishLiabilities.title: SwedishLiabilities
SwedishLiabilities.description: Liabilities are the money that a company owes to outside parties, from bills it has to pay to suppliers to interest on bonds it has issued to creditors to rent, utilities and salaries.

SwedishCurrentLiabilities.title: SwedishCurrentLiabilities

accountsPayable.description: Accounts payable is an accounting entry representing a company's obligation to pay off a short-term debt to its creditors or suppliers.

liabilitiesToGroupCompaniesAndAssociates.description: Current liabilities to group, affiliate or associated company.

otherCurrentLiabilities.description: Other current liabilities then liabilities to credit institutions, accounts payable or liabilities to group comapines or associates.

currentLiabilitiesTotal.description: The sum of all reported current liabilities.

SwedishCurrentLiabilities.description: Current liabilities are a company's debts or obligations that are due within one year or within a normal operating cycle. Furthermore, current liabilities are settled by the use of a current asset, such as cash, or by creating a new current liability.

SwedishLongTermLiabilities.title: SwedishLongTermLiabilities

liabilitiesToGroupCompaniesAndAssociates.description: Long-term liabilities to group companies and associates.

otherLongTermLiabilities.description: Other long-term liabilities then liabilities to credit institutions, accounts payable or liabilities to group comapines or associates.

longTermLiabilitiesTotal.description: The sum of all reported long-term liabilities.

SwedishLongTermLiabilities.description: Long-term liabilities are financial obligations of a company that are due more than one year in the future. The current portion of long-term debt is listed separately to provide a more accurate view of a company's current liquidity and the company’s ability to pay current liabilities as they become due. Long-term liabilities are also called long-term debt or noncurrent liabilities.

SwedishShareholdersEquity.title: SwedishShareholdersEquity

shareCapital.description: Share capital consists of all funds raised by a company in exchange for shares of either common or preferred shares of stock.

sharePremiumReserve.description: A share premium account is the amount of money paid for a share above the cost of the share.

revaluationReserve.description: Revaluation reserve is an accounting term used when a company creates a line item on its balance sheet to account for asset value fluctuations.

otherTiedEquity.description: Contains in most cases of legal reserves, in which the company are required to deposit at least 20% of the nominal value of the share capital.

restrictedEquityTotal.description: Total restricted equity can consist of share capital, statutory reserve and revaluation reserve.

retainedEarnings.description: Retained earnings are the cumulative net earnings or profit of a firm after accounting for dividends.

receivedPaidGroupContribution.description: Received/paid group contribution (excluding taxes) post belonging to total non restricted equity.

receivedGivenShareholdersContribution.description: Received/given shareholder's contribution, post belonging to total non restricted equity.

profitOrLossForTheYear.description: Profit or loss for the year.

totalNonRestrictedEquity.description: The equity that the company can freely dispose of, for example upon dividend.

equityTotal.description:
Total equity.
Equity is typically referred to as shareholder equity (also known as shareholders' equity) which represents the amount of money that would be returned to a company’s shareholders if all of the assets were liquidated and all of the company's debt was paid off.

untaxedReservesTotal.description: Today this post mainly consists of accumulated depreciation in addition to plan and tax allocation reserve.

provisionsTotal.description: Provisions/allocation differ from liabilities by the fact that on the end of accounting period, they should be sucure or probable in their existence but uncertain in amount and/or due date. (While liabilities are usualy secure on due date).

minorityShareholding.description: Holdings that total less than 50% of the share capital or voting rights of a company

SwedishShareholdersEquity.description: Shareholders' equity is the money attributable to a business' owners, meaning its shareholders. It is also known as "net assets," since it is equivalent to the total assets of a company minus its liabilities, that is, the debt it owes to non-shareholders.

netSales.description: Net sales is the sum of a company's gross sales minus its returns, allowances, and discounts.

changeInInventories.description:
Change in inventories and work in progress for the last reporting period and the current reporting period. 

Cost based only

workPerformedForOwnUse.description:
Work performed by the company for its own use and capitalized here when reported as assets.

Cost based only

otherOperatingIncome.description: Other operation income that doesn't count to the normal sales, for example rental income for companys that doesn't conduct property management.

turnover.description: The company’s total revenues. Net sales + otherOperatingIncome

grossProfit.description: Gross profit (loss) in abridged profit and loss account classified by nature of expenses

costOfGoods.description:
Cost of goods sold (COGS) refers to the direct costs attributable to the production of the goods sold in a company. This amount includes the cost of the materials used in creating the good along with the direct labor costs used to produce the good. It excludes indirect expenses, such as distribution costs and sales force costs.

Function based only

sellingExpenses.description:
Selling expense is reported on the income statement as the sum of all direct and indirect selling expenses of a company. It includes all the costs not directly tied to making a product or performing a service. 

Function based only

administrativeCosts.description:
Administrative expenses/costs are the expenses that an organization incurs for administrationcosts and not directly tied to a specific function such as manufacturing, production or sales. These expenses are related to the organization as a whole as opposed to an individual department.

Function based only

rawMaterialAndConsumables.description:
Raw materials and consumables for processing in production.

Cost based only

goodsForResale.description:
Procurement cost for sold gods.

Cost based only

otherExternalExpenses.description:
Other external expenses includes costs for example rent, office supplies, leasing, energy, consumables/inventories, reperations or maintenance.

Cost based only

staffCosts.description:
'Staff costs naturally include salary costs (including social security contributions) but also Recruitment costs, training costs, Costs for staff health care, pension costs and Business health care costs.

Cost based only'

researchAndDevelopment.description: Research and development (R&D) expenses are associated with the research and development of a company's goods or services. A company generally incurs R&D expenses in the process of finding and creating new products or services.

nonRecurringItems.description: A non-recurring item refers to an entry that appears on a company's financial statements that is unlikely to happen again. It represents a one-time expense involving an unpredictable event and is not part of a firm’s normal, day-to-day operations.

otherOperatingExpenses.description: Costs outside of the companys main business and that appropriately can't be placed in other posts in the income statement.

depreciation.description: Depreciation is an accounting method of allocating the cost of a tangible asset over its useful life and is used to account for declines in value. Businesses depreciate long-term assets for both tax and accounting purposes.

operatingExpensesTotal.description: An operating expense is an expense a business incurs through its normal business operations. Often abbreviated as OPEX, operating expenses include rent, equipment, inventory costs, marketing, payroll, insurance, and funds allocated for research and development. One of the typical responsibilities that management must contend with is determining how to reduce operating expenses without significantly affecting a firm's ability to compete with its competitors.

operatingProfitAfterDepreciation.description: The operating profit (loss) is the difference between the business revenue and costs including nonRecurringFinancialItems during the fiscal year.

resultFromParticipationInGroupCompanies.description: The net from dividend income, impairment and reserved write-downs.

interestIncomeFromGroupCompanies.description: Interest payed to the company by group or affiliate.

externalInterestIncome.description: Interest payed to the company by banks or creditinstitutes.

otherFinancialIncome.description: Financial income that is not interest or profit participation from affiliate. For example dividend, profit from bonds or result from stock trading.

interestExpenseToGroupCompanies.description: Interest payed from the company to group or affiliate.

externalInterestExpenses.description: Interest payed from the company to banks or creditinstitutes.

interestExpensesTotal.description: Total sum of interest payed from the company.

otherFinancialExpenses.description: Other financial expenses not covered by externalInterestExpenses and interestExpenseToGroupCompanies.

financialCostsTotal.description: Total financial costs. A sum of all costs.

nonRecurringFinancialItems.description: A non-recurring item refers to an entry that appears on a company's financial statements that is unlikely to happen again. It represents a one-time expense involving an unpredictable event and is not part of a firm’s normal, day-to-day operations.

profitLossAfterFinancialItems.description: Profit/Loss after financials shows the years total result of the business.

extraordinaryIncome.description: Extraordinary income is an unusual earning that results from unusual circumstances unlikely to be repeated. For example, if a corporation sells off idle real estate, this would generate income for the company, but it would be extraordinary, because it’s unlikely to keep selling real estate on a regular basis.

extraordinaryExpenses.description: Extraordinary expenses is unusual expenses that results from unusual circumstances unlikely to be repeated.

extraordinaryItemsTotal.description:
Extraordinary items consisted of gains or losses from events that were unusual and infrequent in nature that were separately classified, presented, and disclosed on companies' financial statements.

extraordinaryIncome - extraordinaryExpenses = totalExtraordinaryItems

resultBeforeAllocations.description:
Earnings before appropriations
or
Result before allocation

groupContribution.description: Given or obtained contribution from/to group or affiliate.

shareholdersContribution.description: A shareholder contribution means that any or all shareholders pay money to the company without obtaining new shares. The motive may be that the owners want to avoid the company having to be liquidated.

appropriations.description: Appropriations is a way of postpone taxes for some of the companys result by allocations to untaxed reserves. The appropriations mainly used is tax allocation reserve.

netProfitLoss.description: A positive value means that the company made a net profit and a negative that it made a net loss.

minorityShareholdingResult.description: Results from holdings that total less than 50% of the share capital or voting rights of a company

floatingCharges.description: A floating charge, also known as a floating lien, is a security interest or lien over a group of non-constant assets. The assets may change in quantity and value. Companies will use floating charges as a means of securing a loan. Typically, a loan might be secured by fixed assets such as property or equipment, but with a floating charge, the underlying assets are usually current assets or short-term assets that can change in value.

propertyMortgage.description: A mortgage is a debt instrument, secured by the collateral of specified real estate property, that the borrower is obliged to pay back with a predetermined set of payments.

otherSecurities.description: Other securitys could be the value of capital insurances or other assets that provides security for pension commitments.

securitiesTotal.description: Securitys or pawns are assets, that explicit or implicit, has been submited in the balance sheet as a security for a loan.

commentsSecurities.description:
F - Securities exist, but amount missing
N - Data not available
Y - Additional securities exist

conditionalShareholdersContribution.description: Contributions from shareholders where the owners have agreed that the contribution should be repaid whenever possible.

otherContingentLiabilities.description: Contingent liability is a potential liability that may occur, depending on the outcome of an uncertain future event. A contingent liability is recorded in the accounting records if the contingency is likely and the amount of the liability can be reasonably estimated.

contingentLiabilitiesTotal.description: The total sum of contingent liabilities. It's commitments that could, but doesn't have to, have economical consequences.

commentsLiabilities.description:
F - Contingent liabilities exist, but amount missing
N - Data not available
Y - Additional contingent liabilities exist but amounts are not specified

salariesAndEmolumentsToBoardMembers.description: Salaries and other emoluments (payments) made to boardmembers.

salariesToOthers.description: Salaries to all other employees except boardmembers.

incentiveWagesToEmployees.description: A monetary gift provided to an employee based on performance, which is thought of as one way to entice the employee to continue delivering positive results. Incentive pay may come in the form of a bonus, profit-sharing, or commission.

socialCosts.description: Social costs for all employees, includes pension costs, payroll overhead and similar.

severancePay.description: Severance pay is the compensation and/or benefits an employer provides to an employee after employment is over.

currentAccountCredit.description: Total credit, given by banks and similar.

usedAccountCredit.description: Credit used, given by banks and similar.

dividends.description: A dividend is the distribution of reward from a portion of the company's earnings and is paid to a class of its shareholders. Dividends are decided and managed by the company’s board of directors, though they must be approved by the shareholders through their voting rights. Dividends can be issued as cash payments, as shares of stock, or other property, though cash dividends are the most common.

type.description:
DEBT_COLLECTION_WITH_NON_PAYMENT_INSTALMENT - Registration of debt collection cases can be made in relation to private individuals if the debtor, within one month of legal action being taken, has not paid the claim, nor expressed that the claim is incorrect. Legal action will usually mean a submission of application for conciliation proceedings, application for writ of execution/attachment, or another type of petition. For enterprises, the same type of registration can be made one month after a payment request has been sent out. Of course, the same reservations regarding settlement and dispute also apply here.
DEBT_COLLECTION - Registration of debt collection cases can be made in relation to private individuals if the debtor, within one month of legal action being taken, has not paid the claim, nor expressed that the claim is incorrect. Legal action will usually mean a submission of application for conciliation proceedings, application for writ of execution/attachment, or another type of petition. For enterprises, the same type of registration can be made one month after a payment request has been sent out. Of course, the same reservations regarding settlement and dispute also apply here.
DEBT_COLLECTION_WITH_CONTINUATED_PROCEEDINGS - Registration of debt collection cases can be made in relation to private individuals if the debtor, within one month of legal action being taken, has not paid the claim, nor expressed that the claim is incorrect. Legal action will usually mean a submission of application for conciliation proceedings, application for writ of execution/attachment, or another type of petition. For enterprises, the same type of registration can be made one month after a payment request has been sent out. Of course, the same reservations regarding settlement and dispute also apply here.
DEBT_COLLECTION_WITH_ACTIONS_FOR_COURT_PROCEEDINGS - Registration of debt collection cases can be made in relation to private individuals if the debtor, within one month of legal action being taken, has not paid the claim, nor expressed that the claim is incorrect. Legal action will usually mean a submission of application for conciliation proceedings, application for writ of execution/attachment, or another type of petition. For enterprises, the same type of registration can be made one month after a payment request has been sent out. Of course, the same reservations regarding settlement and dispute also apply here.
SEIZURE - This is a form of temporary injunction which can be used before the creditor has obtained a judgement for its claim if there are grounds for fearing that ordinary enforcement will be forfeited or impeded to a significant extent. It is largely used in claims against Norwegian citizens residing abroad if the claim is contested.
SEQUESTRATION - A time-limited reaction, in cases where there is a judgement that has not yet been enforced. Primarily this concerns disputed cases, and should not be overemphasized. Since the introduction of the new Enforcement Act, this form of reaction is very rarely used
COURT_JUDGEMENT - A judgement has been rendered by the Conciliation Board. Debtor has either not appeared, or has otherwise expressed that the claim is correct.
CHARGE_DECISION - To secure the payment of fines, repossession, court costs, reparations or redress, which has or is anticipated to be imposed on the accused, the court may, at the request of the prosecutor, impose an encumbrance for a determined sum on assets belonging to the accused, when there are grounds for fearing that enforcement will otherwise be forfeited or significantly impeded. Encumbrance can be imposed until the claim which is to be secured is final and legally binding. A decision by the court cannot be appealed.
CHARGE_ON_PROPERTY - To secure the payment of fines, repossession, court costs, reparations or redress, which has or is anticipated to be imposed on the accused, the court may, at the request of the prosecutor, impose an encumbrance for a determined sum on assets belonging to the accused, when there are grounds for fearing that enforcement will otherwise be forfeited or significantly impeded. Encumbrance can be imposed until the claim which is to be secured is final and legally binding. A decision by the court cannot be appealed.
BAN_ON_DISPOSAL_OF_ASSETS_PRIOR_TO_BANKRUPTCY - Debtor is declared bankrupt, and the bankruptcy court or court of justice has on its own initiative or at the request of a creditor, decided that the debtor's disposal of property which is encompassed by the right of seizure in the case of bankruptcy shall cease. This should be a result of the fact that the court found it likely that the debtor would otherwise dispose of the property to the detriment of the creditors.
TEMPORARY_PRECAUTIONARY_MEASURE - A temporary reaction prior to an enforcement basis being available, which may be equivalent to an attachment, but which only applies when the claim is not a monetary claim.
VOLUNTARY_DEBT_SETTLEMENT - After opening debt negotiations, debtor has been granted a voluntary/enforced debt settlement agreement. The duration of the settlement agreement is normally 5-7 years, and debtor shall be free of debt at the end of this period.
DEBT_SETTLEMENTS_PROCEEDINGS - The Execution and Enforcement Commissioner has granted that the debtor can negotiate with all its creditors in order to, if possible, reach an agreement regarding a voluntary or enforced debt settlement agreement. The announcement of opening of debt negotiations is made in the Register of Mortgaged Movable Property and later followed up by a potential notification regarding an enforced or voluntary debt settlement agreement. However, most debt negotiations end without debt restructuring being implemented.
COMPULSORY_DEBT_SETTLEMENT - After opening debt negotiations, debtor has been granted a voluntary/enforced debt settlement agreement. The duration of the settlement agreement is normally 5-7 years, and debtor shall be free of debt at the end of this period.
NOTHING_FOR_DISTRAINT - This applies to payment requests that are processed by the Execution and Enforcement Commissioner, and when there is presently no possibility of wage garnishment, or judicial registration of lien on movable property or real estate. The petition is returned to the counsel of record as “Nothing to distrain upon”.
DEBT_COLLECTION_WITH_DEDUCTION_IN_SALARY - In connection with an ongoing debt collection case, a fixed wage garnishment is established after the Execution and Enforcement Commissioner's processing of an application for writ of execution/attachment, with the stated amount as the fixed garnishment sum.
DISTRAINT_ON_RENTAL_AGREEMENT - Involuntary lien of debtor's property or real estate. In civil claims, this is a continuation of a debt collection case in which the Execution and Enforcement Commissioner has completed execution proceedings on the basis of simplified treatment of an undisputed claim, or following a legally binding judgement or other enforcement basis.
DEBT_COLLECTION_WITH_PERCENT_DEDUCTION_IN_SALARY - In connection with an ongoing debt collection case, a fixed wage garnishment is established after the Execution and Enforcement Commissioner's processing of an application for writ of execution/attachment, with the stated percentage as the fixed garnishment rate.
DISTRAINT_1 - Involuntary lien of debtor's property or real estate. In civil claims, this is a continuation of a debt collection case in which the Execution and Enforcement Commissioner has completed execution proceedings on the basis of simplified treatment of an undisputed claim, or following a legally binding judgement or other enforcement basis.
DISTRAINT_2 - Involuntary lien of debtor's property or real estate. In civil claims, this is a continuation of a debt collection case in which the Execution and Enforcement Commissioner has completed execution proceedings on the basis of simplified treatment of an undisputed claim, or following a legally binding judgement or other enforcement basis.
DISTRAINT_3 - Involuntary lien of debtor's property or real estate. In civil claims, this is a continuation of a debt collection case in which the Execution and Enforcement Commissioner has completed execution proceedings on the basis of simplified treatment of an undisputed claim, or following a legally binding judgement or other enforcement basis. The same type of involuntary lien is also utilised by the state and municipalities to secure claims for charges such as taxes and value-added-tax. It is worth noting that mortgaging can be implemented to secure a claim where an appeal on the assessment has been submitted. If there are no other negative encumbrances in addition to claims from the municipality treasurer, this registration can therefore be afforded less weight.
DISTRAINT_4 - Involuntary lien of debtor's property or real estate. In civil claims, this is a continuation of a debt collection case in which the Execution and Enforcement Commissioner has completed execution proceedings on the basis of simplified treatment of an undisputed claim, or following a legally binding judgement or other enforcement basis.
COMPOSITION_APPROVED - A publicly announced debt settlement. This is very rarely used, as most businesses now avail themselves of the opportunity to initiate debt negotiations with their creditors without the need for a public announcement. The code can also be followed after a bankruptcy proceeding when there are sufficient funds in the bankruptcy estate so that also creditors who are not prioritised may be paid a dividend from a bankruptcy estate. After the payment, the estate will be relinquished to the debtor, and the operations may continue.

type.description:
SECURITY_IN_AQUACULTURE_LICENCE - Voluntary lien pledged as security for loans or credit in one or another form.
SECURITY_IN_EQUIPMENT - Voluntary lien pledged as security for loans or credit in one or another form.
MORTGAGE_IN_VALUE_OF_DEBT - This is a form of voluntary lien in which debtor's outstanding receivables are pledged as security for a loan, other provision of credit, or transferred to a factoring company as a part of a financing. In the latter case, the company “sells” its receivables, and receives a settlement for them in advance. This way they are covered against losses on claims, but must pay the factoring company a percentage of the nominal value of the receivable. However, most factoring agreements can be equated with other voluntary liens in which the creditor, in addition to, or instead of another type of security, gets the debtor's outstanding receivables as security.
SECURITY_IN_FISHING_EQUIPMENT - Voluntary lien pledged as security for loans or credit in one or another form.

fiscalPeriod.description: Fiscal year-end refers to the completion of a one-year, or 12-month, accounting period. A fiscal year is the period used for calculating annual financial statements. A company's fiscal year may differ from the calendar year, and may not close on December 31 due to the nature of a company's needs.
